speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of Tape 5 with Morris Rubenstein. You have spent a couple of years hiding in the forest, in families' homes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This went on for quite a bit of time, leaving the ghetto. Take me to 1943, 1944. Where were you at this point? Were you still in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I went, I was sleeping under a stick of straw by myself in the morning. And I guy-- I was just fell to sleep. And he told me, he asked me if would go to work. And I worked there maybe for a week, and then I went-- I left. And I left, and I came to a village. And a Jewish girl, you know, she was working outside in the farm. It was a Czech village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said hello, Moshe. Boy, you're grown. You know? And I said hello to her and I left. And then I walked into another farm, and it was a Jewish guy from our city, and I said hello and I talked to him. He survived and his brother survived. And I asked-- What's his name? Lonnie? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then he told-- you know, they brought out a little soup, and I had a little soup and I left. And I walked out and I saw a farmer, a Czech farmer. This was in Poland, I mean in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went like this. I stopped him. He said, who are you? And I told him, I'm Jewish. And he start talking, and he took me to work. And I've worked there, I don't know how long I've worked there. And he didn't need me, so I left. So I left to another farm. Anyway, I'm going to tell you. I went from farm to farm, and this how I've worked. And I've worked in one farm. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. And one time he told me-- one of his daughters told me her brother's a policeman, a Polish policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time, he came. I was working on the farm. And he walked a certain walk, a tall guy. And he said in Polish I shouldn't be afraid. He wouldn't bother me. He talked to me, and then he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone talking about the war coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then I was in another-- in another farm I went. I've worked-- and this was-- the name was [NON-ENGLISH]. A nice guy. And there, you know, a Jewish guy one time he came. And we started talking and he said some of them they came. And he said they hear the artillery. You know? And they say the front isn't too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians, yeah. The Russian front isn't too far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was liberated-- when I was liberated, I was on another farm. And his brother was a Gestapo, a Polish guy. And I was there a few days. And he said the front was coming close. It was coming real close. And they were blowing up the railroad, but it wasn't the Russians. They were the guerrilla fighters. And-- and I was there. I think his name was Vladic. I forgot. And then he came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came in? A Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. The Polack where I stayed there. And he said, [COUGHS], you better go now, just hide in the other room, because his brother might come. And all of a sudden I hear the came and he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Where's [NON-ENGLISH]? Where's the Jew? And he came in, a tall guy. He had the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the swastika and everything. I just looked at him. And it didn't take too long, he left. And then I came out. And I was shaking and everything. So I decided I have to leave him. No. I stayed there. And I think he left them a rifle. And in night, you know, he was watching and then he woke me up, I should watch. They were afraid for the Ukrainians. And they give me the rifle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was shooting on a village, you know, with a Ukrainian there. He was running after me one time. And after I woke him, and he went out to watch to watch, I forgot to close the rifle so it wouldn't-- so when he took it, it misfired. He came and he yelled at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there I think a week. And he went to steal chicken. And he didn't let me go. And after I went back and I-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I stayed there. And I stayed there. The Germans came back. And the Germans stayed there a month. And they put in things. They put in false things so when the Russian airplane come, you know, they wouldn't bomb. They just bomb the first things. But the Russian airborne didn't came. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you excited? Did you think the Russians were coming to free you? Were you hopeful that the Russians were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A lot of Jews, people went-- the underground was there. They went already on the Russian side. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Some Jews went to the Russian side?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Russian side, not too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't know why I didn't go. This was the trouble with me, you know. I could have gone. But one was blowed up. He stopped on a mine, you know. We carried him to hospital after. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. I was right there. And the Germans start going back, the Germans. The Polish, you know, they went away from the-- from the front. And they stayed there. You know? And then a German, a Gestapo, came and he said he's going to blow up everything when they leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in night, I was sleeping and the Germans they came into looking for partisans, for guerrilla fighters. And finally, you know, the Russians came, two Russians. They came and they took the horse from this farm. And I went out and I tell them, why are you taking the horse, you know? He kept me alive and everything. They said, you're talking about this? He said, I'm going to kill my own brother. And you're-- they took the horse and they left. OK. So let me clarify something. You were liberated in March of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town was [NON-ENGLISH]. There was no town. It was a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the day you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day I was liberated, it was terrible, because the German, he came over. And he told me to take a shovel to dig renches, what do you call it? Where they can hide, they can crawl in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the farmer, he went out there, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he said, he's sick. My brother is sick. You know? He can't go. He was afraid, because the other farmers told him, you know, you should tell the Germans I'm Jewish. You know? But he was frightened then. He said, if any of you-- because, it was a lot of Polish people there. They were hiding from the Ukrainians, and a lot of them went away from the front where they were fighting. you know So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And after in the morning, I got up. And I saw already the Germans went away. And I saw the Russians come, you know, on the horses, you know, with some tanks, with some trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel when you saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt real, real, real good. And I've walked with some Polacks, I walked to Rozyszcze. And on the way when I walked, I-- a coat was laying, I picked it up.  I didn't even have a coat on me. I was always barefoot. And then I came to Rozyszcze. There was nobody there. There was nobody there, so I had to go back. I worked on a farm. Then I came back, and it was already Russians there in Rozyszcze and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sick at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ill? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ill, but I didn't think. My mind was different. It was a different-- my personality was different. My whole life was different. I didn't have shoes. I didn't have no clothes. And then I went back. I worked for a Ukrainian. And then the Russians left, and the Ukrainian gave me some food. And he told me he is afraid I should be there, because the bandits are in the forest yet. See, the Ukrainians, they wanted their own country, so they were fighting the Russians too. you know And the Russians were-- so I came back to the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- I came, it was two boys, and it was three ladies there with a little boy. And they were baking bread. They sold it to the Russians. And one boy, he was sick. He was undernourished. Everything. They wouldn't even give him a piece of bread. But what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No, no, no. It's far away. Then I worked in a-- in a mill. Then I went back to-- it was Friday night when they made us soup. So when they cooked the noodles, they didn't give us any soup. But when they cooked the noodles, and they put it through to dry out, they gave us the water from the noodles, the Jewish people. You know? Anyway, from there, I worked in a mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Where did you go next? After you were liberated, did you ever go into a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Rozyszcze almost about five, six months. And I worked in a mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go after those six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the six months, you know, I heard they're going to Israel, you know, and everything. So I went to Lutsk and they said, you know, the people, they can go to Poland, because they're Polish citizens. So I went to Lutsk and I got papers. You know. I walked, and then a farmer drove. I caught a ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got papers from whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Russian KB.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the papers for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The papers were to go to Poland. And I came back, you know. And me and another guy, he lived through in Russia this guy. And I went to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said some people were going to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But in Poland, I came and I didn't have nothing to eat in Poland. So I met another guy and I went to [NON-ENGLISH] in Lublin. You heard Lublin? I stayed there, boy, so long. It would take another 10 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: But no, this is interesting. So how did you get to Lublin? What did you do in Lublin? What happened in Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lublin, I didn't have nothing to eat. It was Pesach. And I didn't eat almost for eight days. I lived on bananas and then a little soup, not to eat bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met a boy in this [NON-ENGLISH] guy. And he said, if I want to go to Lodz. I went to Lodz with him. He said, there you're going to see a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a streetcar. I never saw a streetcar my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went there. We didn't have nothing to eat. And it was him and another guy. They were real-- they broke in where the ghetto used to be. They got something they sold. And then I went to Danzig. And they were still fighting in Danzig, the Russians and the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't get any help from any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No organizations, no nothing. No nothing. Nobody helped me. And in Danzig I got some from the Germans, some clothes, or what. And I went with him to the city what he was. And then it was Polacks where they killed Jews. We were afraid. We just slept over and we came back to Lublin. And I had some clothes from the Germans they sold. Then I myself went back to Danzig. And I traded with the Germans and I brought some clothes back to Lublin and I sold. And then I met a Russian. And we went into a bar and I got drunk. And the farmers were selling things, the Polish farmers. And I went over to them. I turned over everything. I said, you killed the Jews. You killed us, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Russian, you know, he grabbed me. He said, come on, come on. And they're not KB. He said, what did you do? You know? You are soldier. You shouldn't do this thing. I had a Russian uniform on, you know, with a belt and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after, I went to Breslau. And from Breslau, I went to Waldenburg. And I didn't have nothing to eat. I had to buy and sell. Nobody helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any plan or you just kept traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no plans. And then me and another boy, we wanted to cross the border to go to America, so we went to Szczecin, and we thought from there we'd cross to Berlin. So instead of crossing to the American side, we crossed to the Russian side. They put us in jail and finally we went out. And then I want back to Waldenburg. And I went into a restaurant. And it was a Russian soldier and a Polish soldier. And they see me eating, you know. I got some-- and they called me over to the table and they asked me if I'm Jewish. I said yes. They said they're going to open a kibbutz. So I was so tired and everything, so I said yes. We'll open a kibbutz. I go into the kibbutz. That was the first one they organized. And they were Zionist. I guess they were before the war Zionist. They were older guys maybe, in the 40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met these people where again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year are we in now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And I went into the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say a kibbutz--

speaker: interviewee
text: A kibbutz, it was just in a house, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: The plan was I should go and find more boys, and they should come in. We'll go to Israel from Poland. We go to Germany or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you never made it to Israel? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed maybe six months in the kibbutz. We went to another city. And then from there, you know, we went in another city to cross the border to Czechoslovakia. We crossed, we went at night. So we were waiting in a house. And a Polack, Polish soldier, two Polish soldiers-- there must have been maybe 100, 200 Jewish people. And they asked for more money and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, you know, we're not going to give you more money. It's not like it was before. You know? And we start fighting them. Finally, they left. And we waited another two hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went, we crossed the border to Czechoslovakia, you know. It took us maybe from 12 o'clock maybe to 7 o'clock. We came into Bratislava. It was Slovakia, Czechoslovakia. And there, you know, we went in and they put some powder on us. So if it were lice we had, it should kill out the sickness. And we stayed there I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? You stayed where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in what sort of building or what you were in with these--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some kind of building. I think it was the Joint Distribution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you hooked up with the Joint Distribution Committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the Joint. I think so. And then from there, we walked. I had to walk over a bridge. And one side was a chair, a Russian and a Jewish guy. He talked different languages. He said, come, come. He talked to the chair, talked to Russians, and he walked through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Joint Distribution trying to do with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nothing-- now, the Joint Distribution had nothing to do with this. This was Israeli [NON-ENGLISH]. You heard [NON-ENGLISH]? You know, it was an Israeli-- he had to do with it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, did you still think you were going to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel. Just to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with a bunch of other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other survivors, other Jewish. Some were from Russia. Some were from-- and we were walking in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was already Czechoslovakia. Then we walked through the bridge where the Russian was Austria. And we slept over in [NON-ENGLISH]. And in the morning, we start walking to the train, to the railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why were you going to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Vienna, it was another kibbutz there. And we walked there, you know, and we met other people. We divided. And I went to another brigade. It was different brigades, you know? And we went in to a place there. And we had something to eat there. And we stayed there. I think we stayed in Vienna a month or two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we went to see Reichl-- 'Right-shull'? You know, the rich-- what do you call it? Reichl. How do you say it in English? You know, the millionaire he used to make wine and everything? Yeah. So you went there and then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are real, real buildings, you know like all-- and then we went over on the Russian border. And then we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, did you still think you were going to go to Israel eventually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, we went and we crossed to Germany. We had to cross the German border. And we came to a camp, Pocking. You heard Pocking? You know? And there we stayed. We didn't have nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with at this point? You keep saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still with the Brigade Gordonia, a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, maybe we were about 20 or 30. And it was different. It was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is? I had from the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there, we stayed there a month. We were in hunger and everything. And they took us on a farm to teach us how to work on a farm, to teach us how to plow, to teach us how to-- you know, to teach how to milk a cow. Do you see on the horse? This was the kibbutz where I was. And how were you feeling at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt but still I was sick, you know. I was in my stomach and everything, you know like flies fly around. Now from there, we had to go to Israel. So I was so sick and everything. Nobody know what is wrong with me. And I went to take baths and everything. And then they told me I need appendix. So I went to hospital, and there the nurses told me it was a Gestapo he operated on me. I didn't need the appendix. I didn't need nothing. I was just sick, you know, like nerves, or nervous breakdown, or whatever. And while I was sick, you know, and it was a teacher. He taught us Hebrew. So he came in to teach me while I was in bed. And a guy was there. He starts talking. So a guy told him he has relatives in United States. So I said I have relatives in the United States too. And he asked me the name and everything. And I told him. And he said he's going to send it to New York and, they put it in the Jewish news if they can find them. In the meantime, the other one left to Israel. I couldn't go, because I still-- you know, from the appendix.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had someone at the hospital got in touch with a relative of yours in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. Then I went to another kibbutz. And from there, I was going to Israel. Then I came to a DP camp. And I walked through, and they know me there. They said, are you Moshe Rubenstein? I said yes. You have a letter. And I had to go back where I was in the first kibbutz. And they gave me the letter. They found me in the Jewish news. A cousin found me.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cousin found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name was in the Jewish news? Who put your name in the Jewish news?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teacher, but he taught Hebrew. He sent it to New York. And New York sent it to the Jewish news all over the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Relative here in Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the relative's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lindon, Tauba Lindon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tauba Lindon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She died. And the cousins died already too, you know. They were older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you related? From your mother's side or your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my father's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's sister lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were living here. She died, you know, were living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Detroit?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name again was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tauba Lindon. And they asked if I want to come to United States. And I said I want to go to Israel yet. And then they start sending letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I was-- then they want me to go to Israel, so a doctor, a lady doctor checked me, said, are you well? I said, I'm well, I'm all right, but they're just like flies going. So she sent me to a German hospital. And they put in here a long thing and here to check me, a lady doctor, a German. Then they sent me to a clinic and the clinic checked me, looked in the eyes, and sent me to the hospital. And I was there I don't know, a month or two months. And they sent me letters to come. And then they had a cousin here. And they sent him $5 to give it to me and everything. And after one cousin sent a letter, we are trying so hard and everything, and now you're going to go to Israel? So I decided I'll go here to the United States. OK. That's the end of Tape 5 with Morris Rubenstein.
